plain: English is the most common language spoken world-wide and it is one of the easiest languages to learn and speak. This book is all about developing the very basic concept of English grammar. This book talked about the core essentials of English grammar applied in formal English Language. This Grammar book is divided into three (3) essential parts. Part 1 and 2 covers only Grammar lessons and Part 3 covers Composition.

plain: Under the influence of our pseudo educationists and innovators of the so-called structural techniques, the teaching method of formal English grammar in schools have declined in the recent years. The old but sturdy edifices seem to be in danger of disappearing, in favour of less structured methods of teaching literacy. The result of this dangerous trend of grammar lessness are now being felt increasingly, because even after ten to twelve years of schooling, the average student is not able to express himself correctly and fluently in the English language. Grammar forms our basis in the learning of any language, and without lying this foundation firmly the so-called ‘structural approach’ is bound to prove futile and even harmful.

plain: This book is based on the believe that an understanding of the English language must being with a working knowledge of its components - the parts of speech – and with the way they fit together to form the biggest unit of speech, i.e., the sentence. The knowledge given in this book to the student not as an arid exercise, but as a pleasant and rhythmical drill that will enable the beginner to learn this most important language of the world in an easy and enjoyable manner.

plain: The root form or the base form

plain: The form of a word on which its other forms are based is called the root form or the base form.
html: The form of a word on which its other forms are based is called the root form or the base form.

plain: ‘walk’ is the root of ‘walks’, ‘walked’, ‘walking’ and ‘walker’.

plain: In the previous chapter, you have learnt the uses of 'who', 'how', 'what’, ‘when’, ‘where', 'which' and 'why' as question-words. Now we shall study their use as link-words.
html: In the previous chapter, you have learnt the uses of 'who', 'how', 'what’, ‘when’, ‘where', 'which' and 'why' as question-words. Now we shall study their use as link-words.

plain: In the above sentences, ‘there’ appears to be the subject; but the real subject is the noun that follows the verb.
html: In the above sentences, ‘there’ appears to be the subject; but the real subject is the noun that follows the verb.

plain: If the real subject is singular (a table, a clock, some trouble), the verb is singular (is, was, has).
html: If the real subject is singular (a table, a clock, some trouble), the verb is singular (is, was, has).

plain: The word ‘fairly’ and ‘rather’ are both used to express ‘to a moderator degree’.
html: The word ‘fairly’ and ‘rather’ are both used to express ‘to a moderator degree’.

plain: ‘fairly’ is used when the idea is pleasant or positive.
html: ‘fairly’ is used when the idea is pleasant or positive.

plain: ‘rather’ is used when the idea is unpleasant or negative.
html: ‘rather’ is used when the idea is unpleasant or negative.

plain: Tom is fairly cleaver, but Peter is rather stupid.
html: Tom is fairly cleaver, but Peter is rather stupid.

plain: I walk fairly fast, but Anu walks rather slowly.
html: I walk fairly fast, but Anu walks rather slowly.

plain: The speaker can express approval by using ‘fairly’, and disapproval by using ‘rather’.
html: The speaker can express approval by using ‘fairly’, and disapproval by using ‘rather’.

plain: This soup is fairly hot.
html: This soup is fairly hot.

plain: It implies that the speaker likes hot soup.
html: It implies that the speaker likes hot soup.

plain: This soup is rather hot.
html: This soup is rather hot.

plain: It implies that the soup is a little too hot for the speaker.
html: It implies that the soup is a little too hot for the speaker.

plain: Mary is rather tall for her age.
html: Mary is rather tall for her age.

plain: It implies that Mary is taller than we should like, or she is outgrowing her age.
html: It implies that Mary is taller than we should like, or she is outgrowing her age.

plain: Mary is fairly tall for her age.
html: Mary is fairly tall for her age.

plain: It implies that Mary’s tallness is a pleasant fact.
html: It implies that Mary’s tallness is a pleasant fact.

plain: ‘Fairly’ can never be used with a comparative degree.
html: ‘Fairly’ can never be used with a comparative degree.

plain: I did it fairly better last time.			(Incorrect)
html: I did it fairly better last time. (Incorrect)

plain: I did it rather better last time.			(Correct)
html: I did it rather better last time. (Correct)

plain: ‘Fairly’ is never used with colours, unless the colour is modified ‘light’ or ‘dark’.
html: ‘Fairly’ is never used with colours, unless the colour is modified ‘light’ or ‘dark’.

plain: This one is fairly green.				(Incorrect)
html: This one is fairly green. (Incorrect)

html: This one is rather green. (Correct)

html: This one is fairly light green. (= light enough for you.)

plain: This one is rather light green.			(= too light for you.)
html: This one is rather light green. (= too light for you.)

plain: The article a(n) comes before ‘fairly’, but can come before and after ‘rather’.
html: The article a(n) comes before ‘fairly’, but can come before and after ‘rather’.

html: It is a fairly light box.
